mappings.rationaleAI: Twentieth-century Britain covering World War I, interwar social divisions, World War II, loss of empire, welfare state, Thatcherism, and New Labour directly addresses developments in Great Britain (A.1.a) including economic depression and labour unrest (A.1.a.i), colonial and Commonwealth relations (A.1.a.ii), interwar foreign policy and World War II (A.1.a.iii), and postwar Labour and Conservative governments (A.1.a.iv).

British parliamentary democracy, party politics, the Brexit referendum, and constitutional debates connect to postwar political developments including devolved assemblies for Scotland and Wales (A.1.a.iv).

Northern Ireland's political history from partition through the IRA, the Troubles, and the peace process directly addresses Northern Ireland since 1922, growing antagonism between Roman Catholics and Protestants, continuing violence, and the Belfast Agreement (A.1.b.ii).

Modern Ireland covering Home Rule, Eamon De Valera, and Northern Ireland's partition directly addresses the Irish Free State, the Cosgrave and De Valera governments (A.1.b.i) and growing antagonism in Northern Ireland (A.1.b.ii).

France from the Revolution through de Gaulle, Mitterrand, the EU, and colonial legacy directly addresses France since 1920 (A.2), interwar France and World War II (A.2.a), and the postwar period including the Fifth Republic (A.2.b).

Rise of the Nazi party, Hitler's dictatorship, territorial expansion, and genocide directly address the rise of National Socialists and end of the Weimar Republic (A.3.a.i) and the Third Reich (A.3.a.ii).
The 1919 Treaty of Versailles and its impact on European political order directly address the Weimar Republic's reaction to the Treaty of Versailles and reparations payments (A.3.a.i).

Italian history from Risorgimento through the Berlusconi era, economic modernization, and cultural contributions directly address Italy since 1920 (A.4), the Fascist era (A.4.a), and postwar Italy (A.4.b).

Fascism in Mussolini's Italy and Hitler's Germany, far-right movements in Eastern Europe and Spain directly address the rise of National Socialists (A.3.a.i), the Fascist era in Italy (A.4.a), and the Spanish Civil War and Franco (A.5.a.i).
The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939), Franco's victory, and alignment with the Nazi order directly address the Civil War, German and Italian intervention, and Franco's victory (A.5.a.i).

Welfare state systems including social insurance, healthcare, and unemployment protections address the Labour government and welfare state in Britain (A.1.a.iv) and Sweden's welfare state establishment (A.6.b.iii).

Socialism including Swedish social democracy and Cuban communism addresses Sweden's social democratic reforms and welfare state (A.6.b.iii) and the establishment of the Soviet communist state (B.1.a).

Soviet history from revolution through civil war, Stalinist industrialization, Cold War rivalry, and the 1991 collapse directly addresses the Soviet Union from establishment to the end of World War II (B.1.a) and the postwar period (B.1.b).

The 1917 revolutions, civil war, War Communism, New Economic Policy, and rise of Stalinism directly address the October Revolution, establishment of the Soviet government, and the struggle for succession after Lenin's death (B.1.a.i).

Post-Soviet Russian political development under Gorbachev, Yeltsin, and Putin directly addresses Gorbachev's glasnost and perestroika and the dissolution of the USSR (B.1.b.v) and Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus since 1991 (B.1.c).

Soviet economic development from central planning through post-Soviet transition addresses economic recovery under Stalin (B.1.b.i), economic problems under successive leaders (B.1.b), and post-Soviet Russia (B.1.c).

Soviet forced labor system operating 1930-1950s, Stalinist political repression, and 25 million prisoners directly address purges and consolidation of Stalinism (B.1.a.ii) and Stalin's monopoly of power (B.1.b.i).

Communism's global spread, Communist Party structures, and the collapse of European communism address the Soviet communist state (B.1.a), postwar Soviet period (B.1.b), central European communist states (B.2), and the Balkans under communism (B.3).

Habsburg dynasty and multi-ethnic governance across Central Europe provide historical context for Austria (B.2.a), Hungary (B.2.b), and Czechoslovakia (B.2.c) prior to their 20th-century transformations.

EU institutional structure, single market, Brexit, and enlargement address Britain's entrance into the Common Market and subsequent relationship (A.1.a.iv) and Benelux membership and integration in the European Economic Community (A.7).
European postwar reconstruction, Cold War division, decolonization, and social transformation address broadly the nations of western Europe (A) and eastern and central Europe (B) since 1945.

Cold War superpower rivalry, NATO formation, and ideological competition address the postwar Soviet period (B.1.b), central European states under Soviet influence (B.2), and communist governments in Yugoslavia, Albania, Romania, and Bulgaria (B.3.b.ii).

World War II covering German conquests, the Eastern Front, and Allied victory addresses France's defeat and the Vichy government (A.2.a.iii), the Third Reich (A.3.a.ii), Italian participation (A.4.a.ii), Soviet advance into Europe (B.1.a.iii), and German invasion and Axis occupation of the Balkans (B.3.a.ii).

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression and economic crisis provide context for British economic depression in the interwar period (A.1.a.i) and economic instability in the Weimar Republic (A.3.a.i).

Concentration camps under the Third Reich and the Soviet Gulag directly address Nazi persecution of Jews and the totalitarian police state (A.3.a.ii) and purges and consolidation of Stalinism (B.1.a.ii).
Antisemitism from medieval anti-Judaism through the Holocaust and Mein Kampf directly addresses the Nazi revolution, persecution of the Jews, and the totalitarian police state (A.3.a.ii).

Nationalism, self-determination, and ethnic identity connect to the Czechoslovak, Polish, and Baltic struggles for independence (B.2.c, B.2.d, B.2.e) and renewed ethnic conflicts in the Balkans (B.3.b.iii).
The Russian Revolution as a major historical case directly addresses the October Revolution and establishment of the Soviet government (B.1.a.i).
Intrastate armed conflicts, their causes and consequences connect to Balkan civil unrest (B.3.a) and separatist and ethnic conflicts in the former Yugoslavia (B.3.b.iii).

Authoritarian regimes including military, single-party, and personalist types address Italian fascism (A.4.a), the Franco regime in Spain (A.5.a.i), the Salazar regime in Portugal (A.5.b), Soviet authoritarianism (B.1.a), and central European communist regimes (B.2).

Democracy as political system and concept connects to postwar British democratic governance (A.1.a.iv), Czechoslovakia's Velvet Revolution and election of Havel (B.2.c.iii), and movements toward democracy in central and eastern Europe (B.3.b.iii).

Federal power-sharing systems connect to the formation of the Federal Republic of Germany (A.3.b.ii) and reunification (A.3.b.iv), though the primary focus is comparative rather than Germany-specific.

Scotland's devolution, the Scottish Parliament, and contemporary independence debates connect to devolved assemblies for Scotland and Wales within the postwar British political developments (A.1.a.iv).
Forced migration and refugee crises connect to the political disintegration of Yugoslavia and emergence of separatist and ethnic conflicts generating refugee flows (B.3.b.iii).
Terrorism including conflicts in Northern Ireland and Yugoslavia addresses continuing violence in Northern Ireland (A.1.b.ii) and the escalation of hostilities in the former Yugoslavia (B.3.b.iii).

Russian history from Kievan Rus through the post-Soviet era, including the Russian Empire, revolution, and contemporary Russia, broadly addresses the states of eastern Europe section covering the Soviet Union and post-Soviet Russia (B.1).
